And the way that we start generating each article is we provide it with the topic.
The topic is the input, which is typically the title of the article.
And we provide it a brief article direction on what the article should cover.
This is so that the researcher knows what kind of information it should retrieve from the Internet.
And we also explain how it should integrate Talaera into the article so that we ensure that the article actually mentions your product and it's mentioned in the correct way.
And we also provide it with a brief H2, H3 structure.
What we have found is that we just need to control the outline in the way that we need to make sure that it's human-led so that we research the top-ranking content for the target keyword.
And we make sure that our outline.
That we give to the AI matches the search intent of the top ranking content of the target keyword so that we're not creating content that won't rank.
So we provided a brief H2, H3 structure to control the output.
And then once we're happy with the outline, we run the researcher.
And the way the researcher works is that we're using right now Tavali.
And Tavali is a pretty advanced researcher, is more advanced than perplexity for very complex clients with, you know, technical requirements.
We might be using EXA.
And the researcher works by taking the assignment direction and then it analyzes the topic.
And then it creates a list of questions that it needs to answer in order to collect enough information to actually be able to write the article.
And then it creates this very in-depth...-depth...
And C4 profile...
that's that's
Research document that the AI uses as kind of a knowledge base during the generation of the article.
So, for example, some of the critical research questions are to scrape the existing Talaera article and email sign-offs, because this is kind of a refresh.
And then we also have, like, what are Talaera's business English training services?
Like, what are the most professional appropriate email sign-offs, and so on.

And I don't know if you ever tried to use ChatGPT or Claude to create a piece of content, but one of the biggest issues that happens is that the article just kind of talks in circles, and it keeps repeating the same information.
And that's precisely because it doesn't have enough information.
It's very, like, the output is very thin.
And by incorporating this researcher, we really make sure that the article has enough valuable information to actually be insightful.
Then we move forward with the drafting stage.
And at this point, we apply the writing guidelines.
And we will kind of briefly scan this output to make sure that it's following the correct outline, includes all of the right information.
We will add the internal links from your website.
And this is a programmatic step that takes URLs from your sitemap and inserts them into the content.
And we also have a source link step because sometimes the article draft might be referencing specific studies from the research document.

And then in this step, the AI will just add the correct links to make sure to allow us to fact check these claims and also for us to make sure that these links are accurate and take like provide credible information.
Then we have the final.
This proofread step.
And this is where we use the proofreader checklist.
This step is really important.

the past couple of months, I mean, not in the past couple of months, in the last six months, we've been kind of experimenting with how to make this process as efficient as possible while taking into account some limitations of LLMs.
The issue with LLMs is that when you give them a lot of context and a lot of rules at once, they get confused and they will follow some rules but ignore others.
Sometimes a list of instructions might include contradictory steps, and we don't realize that they're contradictory, but to an LLM, they appear contradictory.
So we have the writing guidelines, which are applied at the draft stage, and then the final proofreading checklist takes the draft and then evaluates it against the rules in the writing guidelines.
And then it also implements proofreading rules that are in the checklist, like use sentence case for subheadings, use a full stop at the end of bullet points, things like that.
It really allows us to make sure that the AI has followed the writing guidelines from top to bottom.
And then if there are any parts of the article where the content needs to be rewritten, this step is going to rewrite it so that it's following the writing guidelines.
For example, it might restructure sentences and, you know, just change some phrases.
Then we generate the SEO meta tags, which are the meta title, the meta description, and sometimes we will change this depending on, like, what's ranking and what we think is best.
And then we also run the fact checker, which, the fact checker...
Fathomaker splits an article into different chunks, and then it validates those chunks against the research workflow.

So it pulls out any information that it identifies as needing fact-checking, for example, claims about research reports or best practices, or just claims that, you know, for example, business English learners prefer to have these types of lessons.

Like, those are the types of things the fact-checker would pull out and then validate, and if there's any, if there are any sections where we need additional information, the fact-checker will provide that.
It will link additional sources.
If it cannot find a credible third-party source to authenticate a claim, it's going to rewrite the sentence so that it's not as definitive.
So, for example, it might say...

Although many sources say that learning English in a group setting is a practice, there is no research report to support the claim that, I don't know, 50% of business English learners prefer group lessons.
So that's kind of a sign to us that the fact checker has researched a specific paragraph and that it's rewritten the content so that it's as accurate as possible.
Then we generate the header image using the workflow that we have.
And then the last step is for us to read this article in depth and do a manual review to make sure we catch any additional issues.
We always fact check all of the external links.
We make sure the internal links are correct.
They're not linking to 404s.
We add additional internal links as needed.
And then we package everything in the Google Doc and send it over for your review.

So as I already said, traffic forecasting isn't an exact science because every article ranks for multiple keywords.
So it might rank for dozens of keywords, not just this one target keyword.
And the way that we do this is we take our domain authority.
So your domain authority is 44, I believe.
So that's right in the middle.
And then we calculate the use the expected click through rate for each position.
And this is just based on industry data.
So generally, the articles that rank in position one have a click through rate of 0.3.
And then the further you go down, the lower the click through rate.
But this is also different for each industry.
So, you know, depending on the industry you're in, your click through rates could be higher or lower.
This is just kind of the baseline that we're using here.
And then we calculate, we use these inputs and then to calculate the projected traffic growth.
Our monthly content production is 20.
And this assumes that we're also publishing 20 pieces of content per month.
Yeah, your domain authority is 38.

Yeah, it's kind of a, domain authority is a lagging indicator, so it's not, it's, Google really doesn't use domain authority as a signal.
It's a number that, that different SEO platforms provide, like SEMrush has their own domain authority score, Ahrefs has their own domain authority score, and they, they take into.

account backlinks and then just overall kind of like topic authority and it does like typically if you are publishing content for a longer period of time, the main authority does increase, but it doesn't, the changes aren't very drastic.
